title: Client secrets and privacy

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Client secrets and privacy; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: OWASP Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Client secrets and privacy
sources.claim: Browser JavaScript and delivered configuration are visible to users, so secrets must remain on trusted servers with minimized data collection.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Browser JavaScript and delivered configuration are visible to users, so secrets must remain on trusted servers with minimized data collection.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A browser calls a server endpoint that holds the provider secret instead of embedding the secret in a bundle. Your goal is to review a client design and remove embedded secrets or unnecessary personal-data persistence. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: DOM, events and forms; Promises, async work and HTTP; Web storage and serialization; Fetch and HTTP responses. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Browser JavaScript and delivered configuration are visible to users, so secrets must remain on trusted servers with minimized data collection. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Source maps, logs, analytics, storage, URLs, and third-party scripts can expose data unexpectedly. This draft therefore rejects: An environment variable used during frontend build remains secret after bundling.

includes: Recognize that browser-delivered values are observable and minimize third-party data disclosure.
Review a client design and remove embedded secrets or unnecessary personal-data persistence.
excludes: Source maps, logs, analytics, storage, URLs, and third-party scripts can expose data unexpectedly.

outcomes: Review a client design and remove embedded secrets or unnecessary personal-data persistence.

examples.title: Client secrets and privacy: concrete case
examples.situation: A browser calls a server endpoint that holds the provider secret instead of embedding the secret in a bundle.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A browser calls a server endpoint that holds the provider secret instead of embedding the secret in a bundle.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Anything delivered to browser code is public to that user; keep secrets server-side and minimize exposed personal data.
examples.result: The result follows because Anything delivered to browser code is public to that user; keep secrets server-side and minimize exposed personal data.

examples.title: Client secrets and privacy: boundary case
examples.situation: Source maps, logs, analytics, storage, URLs, and third-party scripts can expose data unexpectedly.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Source maps, logs, analytics, storage, URLs, and third-party scripts can expose data unexpectedly.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: An environment variable used during frontend build remains secret after bundling.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Anything delivered to browser code is public to that user; keep secrets server-side and minimize exposed personal data.
